PURPOSE

These procedures have been compiled for employees of the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police Force) to ensure uniformity in regards to the packaging, safe handling and transport of drugs and drug related exhibits.

The procedures compliment and align with standard operating procedures for Electronic Evidence Recording, Forensic Packaging Guidelines, existing Drug Management Unit (DMU) guidelines, Detective Training School and Foundation Training procedures.

Management Audit Unit (MAU) maintain responsibility for assessing the controls exercised which govern all aspects of drug management. MAU conduct reviews of procedures relating to the receival, storage, auditing, and destruction of drugs. The Drug Management Exhibit guidelines reflect recommendations made by an MAU audit and review of how our agency manages illicit drugs and drug items.

An employee who seizes property is required to make a determination on the evidential value of the item. Consideration should be given for the exhibit to be reduced to an electronic recording, sample, photograph, swab or other forensic application if applicable. An exhibit is required to be disposed of as soon as practical according to policy and legislation.

SCOPE

These procedures specifically relate to the exhibit management and Incident Management System (IMS) submission of illicit drugs and drug items seized by Police. The document has been written to provide practical packaging guidelines in line with drug property management policy and other guidelines. This will assist employees in the correct method of how to retain and submit exhibits via manual handling procedures and electronic data entry onto IMS.

Employees are reminded of their obligations under work place safety regulations.

sections.h:
Drug Management Unit will accept all SME if the item is an illicit drug, a genuine smoking implement, or an item with an approved request for analysis.

Genuine smoking implements should be entered onto IMS as DRUG IMPLEMENTS/ Smoking Implements.

• SME are to be correctly and securely sealed with no holes or punctures, and

no gaps in the sealing strip.

• The SME must be sealed all the way across without deformity.

• Do not use evidence tape on the SME.

• Photograph and body worn camera the exhibit prior to placing your SME into

secure storage.

• One exhibit per SME.

• Only one drug/substance type within an SME.

• Do not mix other articles with drugs inside the SME.

•

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management Each item needs to be separated – no items other than drugs or drug implements to be in the same SME. Drug Types also need to be separated i.e. separate cocaine from cannabis, meth from cocaine etc. Multiple genuine smoking implements from the same IR that are updated for destruction can be placed inside the same SME for disposal.

Place only valid exhibits inside SME.

Use the appropriately sized P11 for your exhibit. Do not use a large P11B or P11C for a tiny amount of drugs or a small smoking pipe.

BARCODE

• Must be attached to the left top side of the SME.

• Must be placed underneath the seal.

• Do not cover the seal with the barcode.

• Do not place the barcode anywhere else on the SME.

• If you make an error or change details, print off another barcode and place the

correct barcode on the SME in the correct position.

INCIDENT NUMBER and PROP REF No: - FILL IN ALL THE BOXES ➢ IR number legibly written in non-smudge ink followed by property reference item number.

➢ You must complete the property item number e.g. 0001 – even if there is only one property item at the time because other items could be later added to the Incident Report.

➢ The Incident Number section should resemble 01012512009134.

➢ Property reference once entered is four digits e.g. 0001 or 0028 or 0125.

EXHIBIT log number if relevant – e.g. search warrant, front counter seizure.

POLICE STATION – insert the correct business area name.

DEPOSITOR / SUSPECT NAME (if relevant)

DATE OF INTERVIEW OR DATE SEIZED must be completed.

sections.h:
• Use correct description of the contents which match the description on IMS.

• The amount on IMS description must match the amount inside SME.

• For example: if you place 3 meth pipes inside SME the IMS amount must

match three meth pipes as well.

• For example: if you seize cannabis in three small clip seal bags and the

weight is 13 grams, place the three clip seal bags inside the that SME, then you must state “three clip seal bags containing green leaf material btb cannabis weight 13 grams” on the SME and onto IMS.

• If you seize a liquid which is inside a syringe or a jar/container, enter the item

as the illicit drug. e.g. 2ml meth inside syringe or 2ml heroin inside small container

• The SME content details must match the description on IMS.

• Do not mix items inside the same SME.

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

• Do not mix tobacco/cigarette butts with illicit drugs.

• Do not place plastic bags/rubbish/containers or other foreign substances

inside an SME with an illicit drug or drug implement.

• Physical evidence collection must be considered, validated and completed

before forwarding the item to DMU.

• Tick the appropriate box for photographs, weight, DFIO and EVR.

• LEGIBLY PRINT YOUR NAME, REGIMENTAL NUMBER, SIGN YOUR SME.

• Seal SME ensuring there are no gaps, wrinkling, holes and binding is secure.

• DO NOT STAPLE THE SME.

BROKEN GLASS – BUBBLE WRAP – FRAGILE ITEMS

• All glass items need to be bubble wrapped

as a minimum.

• Broken glass items must be bubble wrapped

and secured in a sharps/knife container.

• If the item is too large it may be placed

inside a box for safety, then sealed inside a

TAG bag.

• A large glass smoking implement should be

sufficiently bubble wrapped to protect the

thick glass bottom of the item from damage

and from injuring any person.

• A FRAGILE sticker should be applied.

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

CONTAINERS – JARS – GRINDERS - SCALES:

• Containers/jars/grinders/scales are not smoking implements.

• Consider if it is necessary to seize a jar or a container.

• Any jar/container/grinder/scales must be emptied of the illicit drug and the

prohibited substance placed inside a separate SME to the container.

• Any container required as an exhibit is to be placed inside a separate SME.

• Hold the exhibit at the station for 3 months as general property.

• After three months the item is stored at PRESS long term.

• Local OIC can destroy these items.

• Reference WASC Appeal [2023] WASC 445 Rowland v DPP

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

sections.h:
Section 7B of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1981 constitutes what is a drug implement/drug paraphernalia. The Supreme Court of Western Australia appeal [2023] WASC 445 clearly outlines supplementary evidence and parliamentary interpretation required to assess what is drug paraphernalia. Section 7B (6) creates the offence of possessing drug paraphernalia on which there is a prohibited drug or a prohibited plant. Drug paraphernalia is:

(a) any thing made or modified to be used in connection with manufacturing or preparing a

prohibited drug or a prohibited plant —

(i) for administration to a person; or

(ii) for smoking, inhaling or ingesting by a person; or

(iii) to be burned or heated so its smoke or fumes can be smoked or inhaled by a

person;

or

(b) any thing made or modified to be used by a person —

(i) to administer a prohibited drug or a prohibited plant to a person; or

(ii) to smoke, inhale or ingest a prohibited drug or a prohibited plant; or

(iii) to smoke or inhale the smoke or fumes resulting from burning or heating a

prohibited drug or a prohibited plant.

Items such as containers, spoons, knives, bank

cards, sunglasses case, plastic bags, empty clip

seal bags, pestle and mortar, scales, straws,

bread boards, jars, scissors, are not smoking

implements – reference [2023] WASC 445.

These items are general property items. If there is

evidentiary value in seizing these items they can

be stored at your station for 3 months.

Many items can simply be photographed and

recorded in situ with only the illicit item seized.

e.g. a glass meth pipe inside a sunglasses case –

the sunglasses case does not need to be seized

as an exhibit unless a forensic test is required –

seize the meth pipe and leave the sunglasses

case as it is not drug paraphernalia.

Bubble Wrap all glass smoking implements sufficiently to prevent damage to the exhibit and to prevent injury to any person prior to placing inside SME.

If using a sharp safe container bubble wrap the implement where possible to prevent movement and damage to exhibit inside the tube.

Insert paper towel inside an implement to draw out and dry up any fluids. e.g. bong.

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management ELECTRONIC EVIDENCE PROCESS – (Electronic Evidence Recording 1.9)

• When conducting an EER/EVR and placing drug items into smaller

containers, write on the lid of the new container the EER item number.

• The container exhibit lid should record the weight and EER number.

• The combined weight of exhibits inside a SME is restricted to 1kg per SME.

• Multiple containers from the same EER item number can be placed inside a

single SME – each container must be identified and labelled on the top.

e.g. 19FA, 19FAA, 19FAAA containers can be placed inside the same SME.

• Each item within the SME needs to be completely visible and easily identified.

• IMS details must record all the EER number within SME.

(EER 1.9)

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

sections.h:
• Any local forensic process must be completed prior to sending the exhibit to

PRESS or DMU.

• An officer must photograph and body worn camera their exhibit at the scene

or at the station prior to long term storage.

• General property will be retained at the station for three months.

• DMU will accept any correctly packaged item which has been approved for

analysis by FACT.

• The exhibit can be accompanied by the RFA approval document and

transferred to DMU on IMS.

• Instructions need to be updated by the IO prior to transfer.

• ALL RFA approved items must be sent to DMU at the same time.

INSTRUCTIONS for COURT EXHIBITS

• Every property item needs instructions.

• Writing “hold for court” is insufficient.

• Every instruction should contain details outlining the purpose of the item.

• All property items and instructions should be reviewed bi-monthly by the IO.

• Update instructions immediately when circumstances change.

• Once the prosecution matter is completed – the IO is responsible for updating

property instructions for RTO/Destroy/Auction etc.

Court exhibits: - for court exhibits the following at a minimum is required:

• Investigative Officer name and PD:

• Next court date:

• Prosecution number:

• RFA approved Yes or No

LIQUIDS:

Chemicals i.e. methylated spirits, 1,4-Butanediol, etc need to be packaged in a safe way to preserve the integrity of the exhibit and minimise risk to any person.

• No liquids over 500ml per SME.

• Chemistry Centre WA advise the best

method for storage is to use correct

Chemical Bottles (i.e. Schott Jars)

and to seal lid with Parafilm.

• All bottles must be sealed at the top

using Parafilm to prevent leakage.

• Parafilm available on RMIS.

• Call DMU for advice.

• IMS entry type – CHEMICALS (unless

prohibited in MDA Schedules).

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

MOISTURE:

• All excess moisture is to be removed from wet/damp implements.

• Absorbent paper is to be inserted into the implement to prevent spillage and

any further moisture.

• Wet cannabis is to be laid upon moisture absorbent towels and dried out prior

to placing inside an SME.

PLANTS and SEEDLINGS:

• WET plants and seedlings are to be dried out.

• Remove all excess moisture.

• Remove all soil.

• Do not bag plants with soil.

• If soil is required as an exhibit, the soil can be stored at the police station.

• Do not send soil to DMU.

• If the plants are tall, bend in half and insert into the TAG (poly vented) bag.

• The TAG (poly vented) bag must have sufficient room at the top to seal tightly.

• No gaps or holes at the top of TAG bag – secure your exhibits.

• TAG bag not to exceed 15 kg.

• TAG bag must be secured with the TAG number entered onto IMS.

• TAG must be secured with the teeth rachet self-locking.

• A barcode must be attached to a cardboard label then secured to the TAG

itself through the strap or by using string.

• DO NOT USE ELASTIC BANDS TO TIE A BARCODE TO THE TAG.

• All cannabis waste must be updated for destruction prior to transport to DMU.

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

sections.h:
• Prescription drugs or medication should be entered onto IMS as medicine –

not DRUGS.

• It is general property and should be stored at the police station for three

months.

• Can be destroyed by local OIC.

• Coronial medication/drugs should be entered onto IMS as medicine – not

DRUGS – and sent to PRESS for long term storage.

• Coronial medication can be listed as a single IMS entry with the description

panel completed and placed into one storage bag.

• All instructions must be updated.
sections.b: • Prescription drugs or medication should be entered onto IMS as medicine – not DRUGS. • It is general property and should be stored at the police station for three months. • Can be destroyed by local OIC. • Coronial medication/drugs should be entered onto IMS as medicine – not DRUGS – and sent to PRESS for long term storage. • Coronial medication can be listed as a single IMS entry with the description panel completed and placed into one storage bag. • All instructions must be updated.

sections.h:
• Drug requests for analysis for any drug related item will be forwarded to DMU

immediately once approved.

• If the property item is not listed on IMS as a DRUG or IMPLEMENT, select

drug connect tick on property further info tab. e.g. scales that require analysis.

• Send all approved RFA items at the same time to DMU.

• The IO is responsible for every RFA application and item follow up.

• Contact FACT for follow up regarding drug RFA. i.e. certificates. etc.

WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management SOIL and ROOT BALLS:

• No soil to be placed inside a TAG bag or P11 for storage at DMU.

• Soil will not be accepted at DMU.

• Seedling growing cubes. e.g. grow wall will not be accepted at DMU.

• Soil contains microbes, bacteria and insects that jeopardizes the integrity of

exhibits and cannot be introduced to DMU.

• Root balls are generally not seized because they contain less than 0.03%

THC and once severed are not considered prohibited plant material.

• Roots will not regrow once cut at ground level.

• If root balls are seized at the scene they can be destroyed in situ whilst

complying with legislation and policy.

• If an IO decides it is necessary to seize a root ball, the decision must be

articulated on the IMS instruction panel for that item.

• Prior to transport to DMU - all root balls must be dried and packaged

individually to avoid mould, insect activity, and for health and safety risks.

• All root balls must be listed for destruction prior to transport to DMU.

• All Cannabis waste must be listed for destruction prior to transport to DMU.
sections.b: • Drug requests for analysis for any drug related item will be forwarded to DMU immediately once approved. • If the property item is not listed on IMS as a DRUG or IMPLEMENT, select drug connect tick on property further info tab. e.g. scales that require analysis. • Send all approved RFA items at the same time to DMU. • The IO is responsible for every RFA application and item follow up. • Contact FACT for follow up regarding drug RFA. i.e. certificates. etc. WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management SOIL and ROOT BALLS: • No soil to be placed inside a TAG bag or P11 for storage at DMU. • Soil will not be accepted at DMU. • Seedling growing cubes. e.g. grow wall will not be accepted at DMU. • Soil contains microbes, bacteria and insects that jeopardizes the integrity of exhibits and cannot be introduced to DMU. • Root balls are generally not seized because they contain less than 0.03% THC and once severed are not considered prohibited plant material. • Roots will not regrow once cut at ground level. • If root balls are seized at the scene they can be destroyed in situ whilst complying with legislation and policy. • If an IO decides it is necessary to seize a root ball, the decision must be articulated on the IMS instruction panel for that item. • Prior to transport to DMU - all root balls must be dried and packaged individually to avoid mould, insect activity, and for health and safety risks. • All root balls must be listed for destruction prior to transport to DMU. • All Cannabis waste must be listed for destruction prior to transport to DMU.

sections.h:
WA Police Force - Standard Operating Procedures - Drug Exhibits and Drug Management

SYRINGES

• All empty syringes are entered on IMS as MEDICAL ITEMS – not DRUGS.

• One syringe per SME for drug exhibits.

• All exhibit syringes must be packaged separately.

• If a syringe is not an evidential exhibit – e.g. found property item no person

charged – place syringe into a sharps disposal container – no need to enter it onto IMS – can be disposed of at local police station.

• Syringes must be placed into a syringe container needle tube or sharps

container before packaging.

• If the syringe contains a prohibited drug – the item is to be recorded as that

illicit drug. e.g. approx 4 ml meth inside a syringe.

• Syringe sizes are to be accurate. A 10ml syringe cannot hold 20 ml of meth.

• Take care to ensure correct measure of the liquid units and enter this correct

measurement onto IMS.

• Place BIOHAZARD sticker on the SME that contains fluids and needle.

VAPES

• Enter vapes onto IMS as TOBACCO.

• VAPES ARE NOT DRUGS.

• VAPES ARE NOT DRUG SMOKING IMPLEMENTS.

• Contact PRESS for transport and disposal options if required.

• DMU will not accept vapes.

• Contact DMU for queries regarding product content and storage.

sections.h:
SUMMARY ........................................................................................................................ 18
BIBLIOGRAPHY ............................................................................................................... 19
PURPOSE
This précis has been written to offer guidance and assistance to all members of the WA
Police Force (WA Police Force) in relation to police investigations involving The5KIS
(Physical Material) management.
The précis is written to highlight the theory and methodology underpinning ‘Criminal
Investigations’ and are not intended to be referenced as Standard Operating Procedures
(SOPs).
Should information regarding SOPs be sought then readers must refer to the ‘Forensic
Division’ link located on the WA Police Force intranet website under: Home - WAPOL
Business Units > Forensic1.
This précis is an ‘all of agency’ resource but readers should be aware they are specifically
written for the Detective Investigation Training Course.
INTRODUCTION
Modern investigations often rely on forensic processes in which the inculpatory or
exculpatory evidence is at the cellular or even molecular level. It is here, that the importance
of correct exhibits management is vital.
Detectives need to be mindful of the continuity of exhibits for eventual presentation in court.
A comprehensive record detailing the ‘chain of custody’ of each exhibit is fundamental to
criminal investigations. There are numerous instances where prosecutions have failed due
to defence counsel successfully challenging exhibit continuity.
Although many incident scenes are processed by trained Scenes of Crime officers,
occasions may occur when exhibits will need to be collected and managed by Investigating
Officers (IOs).
There are two very different but equally important concepts involved in exhibit management,
the prevention of contamination and continuity of exhibits. For guidelines on how to ensure
both are adequately considered refer to the ‘Forensic Exhibit Handling and Packaging
Guidelines’ produced by the Forensic Training and Development Unit, located on the
Forensic Division intranet site.
1 Forensic Division Homepage http://intranetportal/Home/Forensic/tabid/3784/Default.aspx as 30 May 2019.
CASE STUDY # 1 - Jaidyn Leskie
One of most notorious cases of contamination in Australian Criminal history is that of Jaidyn Leskie.
Jaidyn Raymond Leskie was the one year old child of Bilynda Williams and Brett Leskie. Jaidyn was
kidnapped and murdered in 1997. Jaidyn’s murder remains unsolved; despite leads, the arrest, and a trial
of a prime suspect, who was eventually found not guilty.
Jaidyn was kidnapped from the house of his mother's boyfriend, who was babysitting at the time, in
Newborough, Victoria. The circumstances around his disappearance and death were never clear and were
complicated by a pig's head being thrown at the house and other vandalism on the evening of the toddler's
disappearance.
Jaidyn's body was found on 1 January 1998 at Blue Rock Dam, 30 km north of Moe. His body had been
preserved by the cold waters through the winter and the clothing he was wearing was subject to a DNA
test in an effort to solve the crime. Jaidyn is believed to have died of head injuries.
DNA was located on the toddlers bib and the Victorian Forensic Science Centre matched the DNA to a
sample in a rape case. However the DNA was not a match to the alleged offender in the rape, but was a
match to the victim. The rape victim and her family were questioned extensively by investigating officers
and it was found she had no connection with the murder.
During the 2002 coronial inquest of Jaidyn Leskie, the issue of the DNA result was raised. Barristers
representing the coroner commissioned the assistance of William C. Thompson, a Professor in the
Department of Criminology, Law & Society at the University of California, Irvine.
Professor Thompson closely examined the evidence and with the assistance of several DNA experts,
came to the conclusion there was one of two possibilities that explained the location of the rape victim’s
DNA on the bib of Jaidyn Leskie;
1. The rape victim and Jaidyn’s murderer or some unknown person had exactly the same DNA
profile; or
2. There was either intentional misconduct by laboratory personnel or an egregious laboratory error.
Inter alia, Thompson found the DNA result was a case of cross contamination and the laboratory
processing the exhibits caused the contamination. His detailed investigation could not identify the point of
the contamination but with the absence of any other plausible scenario it was deemed the two exhibits,
which were processed in close proximity in time to each other, had exchanged DNA material.
This case indicates just how easily the contamination of an exhibit can occur and gives foundation to the
principles of exhibit packaging and exhibit preservation.
See footnote 2 for reference to the report of Professor Thompson.
2 W C Thompson, Victoria State Coroner’s Inquest into Death of Jaidyn Leskie (2003)
<http://www.bioforensics.com/app/download/5286528/Kranereport.pdf > at 19 October 2017.

sections.h:
The Butterworths Australian Legal Dictionary defines an exhibit as an object received in evidence when tendered by one of the parties to a case3. As a purely legal definition, this is accurate but this definition does not reflect what an exhibit is, from a police perspective. It may be more accurate to describe an exhibit as an item taken into police custody for possible forensic examination and or tendering in court in accordance with the rules of evidence. It is for this reason the WA Police Force has adopted the term ‘physical material’ to describe items seized during an investigation.

As can be seen, the procedures applicable to exhibit packaging will not only apply to those items that will be presented in court but also those that may be presented in court. It is imperative detectives treat all seized physical material the same and there is no compromise in the quality of the packaging or the way in which the physical material is dealt with.

sections.h:
The WA Police Force Forensic Division has authored a number of procedures when collecting seized physical material, in particular, that which may need to be scientifically examined4.

Whenever physical material is handled, it is imperative that gloves are worn. A new pair of gloves should be worn for every item to avoid cross contamination (see inset5).

Physical material should be handled

as little as possible. If a forensic

examination is to be conducted, the

more often physical material is picked

up, moved or examined, the more

opportunity there is for microscopic

amounts of trace evidence to be lost.

Once collected the physical material

should be stored in a secure place.

The less accessible the physical

material, the less defence counsel can

argue contamination has occurred.

Protect physical material from heat,

light and dampness. Exposure to any of the aforementioned can cause the loss of evidence.

Deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA), although it is a very stable molecule, can be degraded by heat, light, time, humidity, fungi and bacteria6. Fungi and bacteria can flourish in damp environments.

Physical material should be clearly labelled at all times. WA Police Force Forensic Division have adopted a minimum standard regarding the information contained on the label (see inset). The label should reflect the following information.

Detectives should also include the exhibit log number on the label for ease of cross referencing later in the prosecution process.

Labelling should be directly written on the packaging not onto an adhesive label as labels can fall off during storage.

A biological/chemical hazard label shall be attached to any evidence package or container that contains evidence which may present a hazard. The label should be attached in a prominent position to warn any person who handles the physical material of the potential danger.

Initial labelling of physical material should be completed at the time of collection. Additional information should be entered onto the label once the physical material has been recorded onto the IMS system, this information should include the IMS property item number. It is important all physical material is labelled the same way to ensure consistency and accuracy.

Remember it is always better to seize physical material than it is to only take a swab. The recovery of biological material is more likely if the entire item is seized. If there is a possibility of trace evidence being lost in either the packaging process or any other process prior to examination, investigators may consider taking a swab of the physical material prior to packaging it. It is recommended this course of action be only undertaken after seeking advice from a suitably qualified scene of crime officer.

Physical materials should be photographed in situ. This will ensure the context of the seizure is captured. Remember, once physical material has been moved, it should not be replaced then photographed, as the evidence offered regarding its original location may be excluded.

Investigators should be aware of Professor Edmond Locard’s principle of exchange. sections.h:
Physical material should be packaged immediately upon collection. Allowing numerous items to be placed in close proximity to each other could cause cross contamination of the physical material and allow challenges by defence counsel as to the integrity of the evidence.

7 T Jalanti, P Henchoz, A Gallusser and M S Bonfanti, ‘The persistence of gunshot residue on shooters' hands’ (1998) Science & Justice 39, Issue 1, 48-52.

Packaging should be used that is appropriate to the nature of the physical material and only one item per container. The type of packaging will be dependent on the circumstances.

Consideration should always be given to ensure the physical material is protected from damage, that the risk of disruption to trace evidence is minimised, elimination of possible contamination and the risk of exposure to biological or chemical hazards to persons handling the physical material is eliminated or reduced.

Only approved types of packaging are to be used for items collected from an incident scene.

For a comprehensive description of handling and packaging exhibits refer to ‘Forensic Exhibit Handling and Packaging Guidelines’ located on the Forensic Division intranet site.

Packaging is only one component of effective physical material management. The most appropriate packaging may be selected but if it is not sealed correctly the likelihood of contamination is greatly increased.

It is imperative that correct methods and devices are used to seal all types of physical material packaging.

When identifying what items are to be seized, it is important to evaluate what evidentiary value they have. During this evaluation process it is better to err on the side of caution. It is a simple process to later return an item to an owner, but once you have left it, the integrity is lost. Put simply; it is better to have seized it, than wished you had.

CONTINUITY

Seizure is simply the beginning of the management of physical material. If physical material is to be tendered in court, then continuity and a record of that continuity is paramount.

sections.h:
The recording of physical material onto a log is the first step in recording the ‘chain of custody’. The importance of accurate recording and completion of the log cannot be over stated.

Investigators should be familiar with the WA Police Force Interim Property Receipt/Physical Material Log, however the knowledge of how to accurately complete it will vary. Consistency in this area will greatly benefit the investigator and the organisation as a whole.

With the exception of those fields relating to the property seized, the exhibit log has a number of compulsory fields that should be completed and each will be addressed individually.

 Location: This field should be completed with the actual location the search is conducted. If there is not a specific address, then it should be completed with some sort of reference point i.e. Brand Highway, two kilometres north of the Cataby roadhouse. This will ensure there is no confusion as to the place the item was seized.

 Occupier/Witness: The name of a person who has responsibility for the place. They may be the resident or a person who has some tangible connection. If an independent senior officer is used, then their details should be recorded here.

 Date: Record date of seizure.

 Arrival Time: Record time of arrival at location.

 Entry Time: The time entry was made, not the time the search commenced. This may also differ from the arrival time.

 Departure Time: The time police departed the premises, not the time the search ceased. It may only be possible to have this time recorded on the police copies as the witness copy may be afforded to them during the closure of the search.

 Page Numbers: It is imperative page numbers are recorded so there can be no allegation more items were seized during the search. The complete number of pages ensures finality to the seizures. After the last entry relating to the property seized, on the last page, a diagonal line should be ruled from the bottom of the last entry in the ‘Physical Material’ number cell, to the bottom right corner of the ‘Distribution’ cell. In that way, no additional entries can be made by any party.

 Operation/Incident: This should be filled out if there is a specific operation name associated with the search or a brief description of the type of incident.

 Investigating Officer: The Investigating Officer’s (IO) details should be recorded here.

 Physical Material Officer: If an officer is specifically assigned the management of exhibits (physical material) then that officer’s details must be recorded here.

 Video Camera Officer: The details of the officer designated with recording events via video and the taking of photographs.

 Forensic Register Reference: Unique number generated when the forensic register is utilised. Normally applies to qualified forensic officers.

 PFA Declared: Protected forensic area declared (yes/no/n/a).

 Time Declared: Time the PFA was declared.

 Physical Material Officer Signature: The signature of the designated Physical Material officer for the duration of the search.

 Occupier/Witness Signature: This signature should be that of the occupier/witness listed previously. If the person refuses to sign, then ‘Refused to Sign’ (RTS) is acceptable.

 IMS Reference: This IMS report number should be specific to the search and not a general number associated with an offence.

 Property Returned: Date/time of property return.

 Occupier/Owner: Name of person to whom property was returned and signature.

 Witnessing Officer: Signature of officer returning property (with legible rank and PD number included).

Although the completion of the above details is important, the recording of the detail of the physical material seized is more so. Do not restrict yourself to a single line if more are required.

 Physical Material No: As detailed earlier, the item number should not just be a numeric list, it should comprise of the exhibit officers initials accompanied by a number sequence starting at 1 (see figure 1).

 Description of Physical Material Include Photography Taken: This is possibly the area that is routinely not given its due attention. A detailed description of the item needs to be entered. Entries such as “a knife” are inadequate. A more appropriate entry may be “a black handled kitchen knife, approximately 30 centimetres in length with a serrated blade”. A detailed description will ensure there is no confusion between items.

 Specific Location: Another area routinely neglected is the location of where the item was located. All too often entries such as “bedroom” are recorded. Vague entries such as this can cause difficulties during trial when the specific location of an item is in issue. Insufficient detail will allow increased opportunities for challenge by the defence. A more appropriate entry in this section may be, “Bedroom one, south east corner of house, towards rear of the second drawer of the dressing table”. Associating the location point with a sketch of the scene may also prove to be beneficial.

 Item Located By: This specifies the officer who located the item, regimental number or surname are suitable however regimental numbers are unique and do not allow for any discrepancies, therefore regimental number is the preferred option.

 Photography In Situ: Yes/No

 Conveyance Movement Envelope: The conveyance bag is a security movement envelope is used to initially secure valuable items or drugs before processing/weighing and separating into other bags.

 Security Movement Envelope: This section can be used in two ways; if a drug is seized at the scene and no other process is necessary i.e. weighing, then it should be secured in a security movement envelope and the number recorded here.

Alternatively, if processing or weighing is required then the number of the final bag used should be recorded.

 IMS Item No: The IMS number property reference number (a four digit number i.e.

0001) should be recorded here once the item is recorded onto IMS.

 Distribution: This section relates to what has happened to the physical material at the completion of the process. Options include, but are not limited to, return to owner (RTO), sent to Chemistry Centre for analysis, forwarded to Exhibits Management Unit

(EMU) or storage at office.

Investigators will be well served to complete the exhibit log in its entirety and with as much detail as possible. It will serve as a valuable tool during the trial process and unequivocally establish the initial ‘chain of custody’. The detailed completion of these sections of the exhibit log will prove to be of great benefit later in the process. The passage of time can alter recollections of events, information recorded at the initial stages can serve as an aide memoir later.

sections.h:
Upon return to the office, seized physical material is to be recorded onto IMS. IMS is the primary case management system and other case management systems such as VIPER should be used in conjunction with IMS.

The recording of the property onto IMS forms the next step of the recordable ‘chain of custody’. It is very important the property is recorded correctly and accurately to ensure there is limited opportunity for the loss or damage of evidence while in storage. Corporate information (Police Manual PR – Property and Drugs)8 exists on the procedures for recording of physical material within IMS; therefore detailed instruction in this area is not necessary.

Investigators need to be aware that once recorded onto IMS, the property is held within a system that will form the basis of the ‘chain of custody’ for the remainder of the investigation.

There are several areas that have the potential to be overlooked when recording property onto IMS, these areas can be beneficial in ‘chain of custody’ issues; these shall be touched on briefly.

 Exhibit Reference: The specific exhibit number the item was given should be recorded here. As this is a unique reference it will assist in identifying the item throughout the process, thus minimising confusion and error.

 Interim Receipt Number: The receipt number printed onto the Interim Property Receipt / Physical Material Log is to be recorded under the ‘further info’ tab. This will assist in cross referencing.

The above areas may appear to be mundane and without great benefit, however evidence of continuity can become quite convoluted and the benefit of utilising a system where each record is complimentary of all others is self-evident.

There are timeframes applicable to the recording of property onto IMS and these are detailed within the Police Manual PR-01.01.3 Recording and Entering Property in Police Possession.

Those timeframes are always use:

 For high risk items of jewellery, money or perishables, within 12 hours or prior to ceasing duty, whichever comes first; and

 For all other items, within 24 hours or prior to ceasing duty at the end of the following shift, whichever occurs first.

8 Corporate Knowledge Database: Police Manual (PR – Property and Drugs) http://prd-knowledge/html/knowledge/cops/knowledge.htm

sections.h:
Once physical material is recorded within IMS there are initially two paths it will take, either it will be stored securely or it will be transferred to another area for examination. The requirements for each option will be dealt with separately.

Storage of physical material should be in an approved secure location within a police station (inclusive of district forensic office locations) and should be for the purposes of preliminary examination or pending conveyance to the EMU or other long-term storage area.

All evidence deposited in or removed from the short-term storage area for the purpose of alternative storage requirements (long or short), will be recorded in the evidence log (IMS Property report or running sheet) and the Unified Physical Material List (UPML).

Such notation shall include, but not be limited to, the following details:

 Date of deposit/removal.

 Property tracing number (IMS Number).

 Number of items deposited.

 Depositing member.

 Removing member.

When an item is not being examined, it is to be stored in an approved secure storage location.

When making an assessment regarding the storage of physical material, the assessment should be made on an individual basis. Consideration needs to be given to possible environmental factors that may affect evidence and also security factors may be instigated to ensure there is no impropriety; malicious or otherwise.

Should an item require scientific examination, analysis must first be approved by the Forensic Analysis Coordination Team (FACT) via the Request for Analysis (RFA) process9.

All physical material forwarded to EMU for analysis by the Chemistry Centre or PathWest are to be accompanied by a FACT approved RFA form.

Physical material that requires examination by an external laboratory must be sent to EMU promptly after the RFA is approved by FACT. Failure to do so may result in unrealistic time frames for testing, and may result in evidence not being available to a court. Written requests from ODPP for further testing of physical material should be forwarded directly to FACT.

EMU is responsible for delivering and collecting all physical material from laboratories.

Police Officers shall not convey physical material requiring analysis directly to a laboratory without permission from the OIC FACT.

When physical material submitted for an analysis subsequently becomes superfluous, it is imperative the OIC FACT is notified so the analysis can be cancelled. This ensures laboratories are not inundated with unnecessary requests.

Often investigators want to send every item for analysis, however an evaluation of each item must be conducted to establish evidentiary value and what analysis is required. Further assessment of the potential for forensic value from physical material should be conducted in conjunction with FACT. These steps will have the effect of ensuring only the necessary items are sent for analysis, reducing laboratory workload, allowing for faster processing and the potential to receive results quicker.

Physical material should be prioritised as to which is of greater value to the investigation;

i.e. physical material that is fundamental to the identification of the offender would typically be of higher priority than those that support the events in question. This determination will be unique to each investigation and a detailed interpretation of the incident scene will assist in this area.

9 Business Information Systems . (2017, November 2). RFA Procedure. Retrieved from Self-Help HQ:

https://selfhelp/Pages/How%20to%20-%20RFA%20Procedure.aspx

sections.h:
When the physical material is required to be transported, a record must be made of when it was removed and for what purpose. Records should be in both a running sheet and on IMS and include all information enumerated in the above list. The UPML should at all times reflect the current status and location of the item.

Unless exceptional circumstances exist, physical material shall not be transported unless contained in sealed evidence bags or containers.

Prior to transport, an assessment shall be conducted of each item, ensuring the transport method selected is suitable to guarantee its integrity.

The property movement documentation should accompany the physical material during transport. Appropriate paperwork will reduce the risk of confusion at the point of receival.

When transporting physical material subject to an IMS report, it is the conveying officers’ responsibility to ensure at all times, the location of the physical material is accurately reflected on the system.

Whenever physical material is moved from one location to another the actions taken are to be accurately and diligently recorded onto the IMS property movement screen. The movement should also be recorded onto the investigation running sheet and the UPML.

Where large bags containing multiple physical materials are conveyed to EMU for storage, the bag must clearly display a record of each item contained therein.

Following transport, an up to date copy of the IMS receipt and UPML should be added to the appropriate case file showing amended instructions and the new location of the item.

An appropriate entry on IMS showing the new location of physical material should follow any transport.

The recording of physical material movement, which eventually forms the ‘chain of custody’, is done for one purpose - to enable the physical material to be tendered as evidence.

CONTINUITY SCENARIO (Part 5)

The bedding is subsequently conveyed to PathWest for analysis. The item is received as per procedure and stored pending analysis.

At a later date one of the scientists at PathWest takes the item and commences the preliminary examination in preparation for the DNA analysis.

Continued later.

The physical material cannot be tendered by the production of supporting documentation i.e. exhibit, IMS reports or running sheets. It must be tendered via a witness.

For an exhibit to be tendered, its entire ‘chain of custody’ must be proved. Therefore all persons involved in the movement, examination and storage of the exhibit must provide a statement. All too often, a statement of this nature is just referred to as a continuity statement and little understanding of its importance is realised.

CONTINUITY SCENARIO (Part 6)

The bedding is subsequently conveyed to the DNA analyst. The item is tested and it is found to have the DNA of the Suspect on it.

The item is subsequently returned to EMU and later returned to the office of origin.

Every statement detailing the movement of an item is fundamental to prove the ‘chain of custody’. An inaccuracy in any link of the chain may ultimately affect its admissibility.

Therefore, it is imperative each statement accurately details the movement dates, times, reason and officer/s involved. Only then is the successful tendering of the exhibit in court assured.

Throughout this section there has been an unfolding scenario. The reason for this is to demonstrate the possible complexity of physical material continuity. How many statements are required for this physical material to be successfully tendered in court? Given what has transpired throughout the scenario and at both EMU and PathWest the person receiving the physical material is different to the person securing the physical material there are no less than 18 statements required, all for the tendering of this one exhibit. The statements required are listed in the below chart.

EMU Statements

 Receival

The above ‘chain of custody’ is not dissimilar to the production of most exhibits. A record of a convoluted ‘chain of custody’ is easily established if the investigator has accurately recorded the movements of the physical material within the documentation and established systems referred to earlier.

In addition to an easy reference to exhibit movements, accurate recording of these movements will serve to corroborate the witness’s testimony.

sections.h:
The number of witnesses required to tender exhibits can be extensive and will form a part of the brief of evidence. As such, they will be included on the Unified Witness List (UWL) and potentially the UPML. Often at trial, many witnesses that form part of the continuity process will be conceded by the defence. Detectives must remember this is a result of consultation between the prosecution and the defence and not a ‘fait accompli’. The investigation and resulting prosecution brief must include all continuity witnesses and not anticipate continuity witnesses will be conceded.

In fact, often there is confusion as to which witness will tender an exhibit. The vast percentage of the time, the exhibit will be tendered by the police. The exception may be where the exhibit is subject to a subpoena and never in police control.

There is anecdotal evidence, many officers believe exhibits must be tendered at an early stage of a trial to be referred to in evidence. This is incorrect; the entire ‘chain of custody’ needs to be proved before the exhibit can be tendered. This does not prevent it from being shown to a witness, as it may be ‘marked for identification’. This essentially means the witness is shown the exhibit; it is identified but not tendered. It is tendered once the entire ‘chain of custody’ is proved.

Therefore, the standard rule applied is the exhibit is tendered by a representative of the organisation that has custody of it at the time of the trial (predominantly the police). All other witnesses are shown the exhibit and they identify it as the item relative to their testimony.

sections.h:
OSH applies to the management of physical material from its initial seizure to its ultimate disposal. Many items seized by police are dangerous to the safety of both the seizing officer and all persons having subsequent contact with the item.

Emphasis must be placed on risk minimisation. The most common OSH issues include:

 Handling of physical materials;

 Dirty/dangerous environments; and

 Biological, chemical, structural, electrical, and postural hazards.

Personal protective equipment (PPE) must be worn when handling physical material. PPE can protect you from injury or illness as a result of handling physical material, it can also prevent you from contaminating the item. PPE includes but is not limited to, disposable overalls, masks, safety goggles, boot covers or gloves.

It is the responsibility of all officers involved in the physical material management processes to ensure the risk of injury and or health issues to any person involved in the handling of the physical material is minimised. This will be accomplished by correct labelling, packaging, handling, storage and the use of PPE appropriate to the risk.

See footnote10 for references for the case study.

SUMMARY

Physical material is an integral part of the majority of investigations. The correct packaging, transport and storage procedures must be complied with in order to ensure the integrity of potential exhibits. The nature of many forensic sciences is delicate and to ensure a successful examination is conducted, investigators must be mindful of the correct procedure in order to minimise the chances of contamination.

For exhibits to be tendered in court, it is imperative the ‘chain of custody’ is documented and record can be subsequently submitted to the court to ensure the exhibit can and will be accepted into evidence.

sections.b: OSH applies to the management of physical material from its initial seizure to its ultimate disposal. Many items seized by police are dangerous to the safety of both the seizing officer and all persons having subsequent contact with the item. Emphasis must be placed on risk minimisation. The most common OSH issues include:  Handling of physical materials;  Dirty/dangerous environments; and  Biological, chemical, structural, electrical, and postural hazards. Personal protective equipment (PPE) must be worn when handling physical material. PPE can protect you from injury or illness as a result of handling physical material, it can also prevent you from contaminating the item. PPE includes but is not limited to, disposable overalls, masks, safety goggles, boot covers or gloves. It is the responsibility of all officers involved in the physical material management processes to ensure the risk of injury and or health issues to any person involved in the handling of the physical material is minimised. This will be accomplished by correct labelling, packaging, handling, storage and the use of PPE appropriate to the risk. See footnote10 for references for the case study. SUMMARY Physical material is an integral part of the majority of investigations. The correct packaging, transport and storage procedures must be complied with in order to ensure the integrity of potential exhibits. The nature of many forensic sciences is delicate and to ensure a successful examination is conducted, investigators must be mindful of the correct procedure in order to minimise the chances of contamination. For exhibits to be tendered in court, it is imperative the ‘chain of custody’ is documented and record can be subsequently submitted to the court to ensure the exhibit can and will be accepted into evidence. 10 M M Houck et al, as above n 5, 46.
